Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty inconsistent. I'd try to set aside time on weekends to watch the lecture videos, since I'm a visual learner and those helped me understand concepts better. But with my extracurricular activities taking up a lot of energy, I often ended up studying in random bursts whenever I could find a moment. I'd download materials and review them on my phone during commutes since I don't have reliable internet at home.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot. I'd have good intentions to study at the same times each week, but then something would come up with my activities or I'd just feel too drained. Deadlines were really what pushed me to actually sit down and focus. Without that pressure, I'd often let days slip by without doing much coursework.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I remembered there was a deadline coming up, or when I wanted to watch the video lectures. Sometimes I'd get a notification about new content and that would prompt me to check in. I also logged in to see if there were any announcements or if my peers had posted anything interesting in the discussions, though I rarely contributed myself.
Q4: For a while, I got into the habit of logging in every morning just to check what was new. It became almost automatic, like checking social media. But that routine fell apart when my stress levels started climbing and I had more going on outside of class. After that, my logins became more sporadic and reactive rather than proactive.
Q5: The video content was definitely the most useful for me since I learn best by seeing things explained visually. I spent a lot of time watching those lectures, sometimes rewatching parts I didn't fully understand the first time. The visual diagrams and demonstrations really stuck with me better than reading text-heavy materials.
Q6: I mostly ignored the discussion forums and the reading materials. I only posted once because I felt like I had nothing valuable to add, and the readings felt overwhelming when I was already struggling with time. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never really came. The text-based stuff just didn't engage me the way videos did.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it helped something click in my understanding. The videos felt worthwhile because I could see concepts being demonstrated. But if something felt like busywork or if I couldn't see how it connected to what I needed to know for exams, I'd deprioritize it.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt like extras I could skip when things got busy. Required ones had deadlines attached, so they created urgency. With optional stuff, I'd think 'I'll do this if I have time,' but with my low motivation and high stress, that time never materialized. It's not that I didn't value them, I just couldn't justify them when I was already behind on required work.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download the videos and watch them later when I didn't have internet access. I also took handwritten notes from the videos and studied those separately. Sometimes I'd look up additional explanations on YouTube or other sites when I was somewhere with wifi, then save those for later too.
Q10: There were many times I was reviewing my handwritten notes or going through downloaded materials on my phone without being connected to the LMS at all. I'd spend hours with my notes spread out, trying to make sense of things, but none of that would show up anywhere. My actual study time was probably higher than what the system recorded.
Q11: Yes, this was pretty common for me. I'd access a video once, download it, and then watch it multiple times offline over the following days. The LMS would only show that single access, but I might have reviewed that same content three or four times. Without home internet, this was really my only option for repeated study.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. My stress was really high, and I felt like I was falling behind in everything at once. My extracurricular commitments didn't slow down, and I just couldn't find the energy to engage properly with the course. Even when I logged in, I felt overwhelmed by how much I'd missed.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from my other activities or stressed about life stuff, the LMS became something I'd avoid because it reminded me of all the work I wasn't doing. The fatigue made it hard to concentrate even when I did log in. I'd open a video and realize I'd zoned out through half of it.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have the LMS open but I wasn't really there mentally. I'd be watching a video but thinking about other things, or scrolling through materials without actually processing anything. My mind would wander, especially when my stress was peaking. The engagement looked real on paper but it was pretty hollow.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed and not knowing where to start. Part of it was low motivation making everything feel like a mountain to climb. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with the same excuses. It's a cycle I couldn't seem to break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. My LMS activity would spike right before something was due. I'd cram to get things done, which probably hurt the quality of my work. Without a deadline looming, I'd drift and do other things. It wasn't a healthy pattern, but it was my reality.
Q17: I didn't really get much feedback early on, and when I did get it later, I was already feeling pretty defeated. Feedback might have helped if it came sooner and more regularly. By the time I saw my scores, I felt like it was too late to turn things around, which just made my motivation drop even more.
Q18: The course structure was pretty open, which sounds good but actually made it harder for me. I needed more guidance and checkpoints to stay on track. With my low motivation, the freedom to manage my own pace meant I fell behind quickly. More structured deadlines throughout might have helped me stay engaged.
Q19: The video content was great for my learning style, so that helped. But the lack of structure and the heavy reliance on self-direction made things harder. Also, not having consistent internet access meant I couldn't engage with live or interactive elements. The asynchronous nature helped somewhat, but I still struggled with the overall design.
Q20: I can relate to that actually. Sometimes you're logging in, watching videos, exploring materials because you want to understand, but when it comes to actually completing and submitting assignments, there's a disconnect. Maybe it's anxiety about doing it wrong, or feeling like you're not ready. The activity shows effort, but the output doesn't match.
Q21: That makes sense to me too. If someone studies effectively offline and just uses the LMS minimally to grab what they need, they could still do well. I tried to do this but my exam performance didn't reflect my effort. Maybe for some people, less LMS time but more focused offline study works better than constant logging in.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active, how long you spend on videos, and what you click on. But they miss the quality of that engagement and all the offline work. They can't tell if you're actually learning or just going through the motions. They definitely can't capture the stress and life circumstances affecting your engagement.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just checking boxes. It's when you're curious and want to learn more, when you think about the content even outside of study time. For me, true engagement happened during those moments when a concept finally clicked while I was reviewing my notes alone. It's internal, not just about activity metrics.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much time I spent with downloaded materials offline, or how my stress and low motivation were constantly battling against my desire to do well. It wouldn't show the CBT sessions I had to help manage my mental state, or how my extracurricular commitments competed for my energy. The numbers tell a story, but it's incomplete.
